subject: English

lesson.story_mission: Join the Word Detectives to unlock a library gate by reading bigger Grade 2 words.
lesson.objective: I can decode words with blends, digraphs, silent e, vowel teams, and two syllables.
lesson.concept_explanation: Strong readers look for familiar spelling patterns before blending a word. In a consonant blend, each consonant sound can be heard. In a digraph, two letters spell one consonant sound. Vowel teams and silent e can signal a long vowel. Longer words can be divided into spoken syllables or meaningful compound-word parts.
lesson.common_misconception: A reader may reverse letters in a blend, split a digraph or vowel team, pronounce the final e, or divide a two-syllable word where the spoken beats do not break.
lesson.real_world_connection: These word-analysis moves help readers solve new words in stories, directions, science books, and classroom signs.
lesson.lesson_summary: Spot the pattern, mark the sound chunks, blend the word, and check the meaning.
lesson.mini_lesson: First scan the whole word for a blend, digraph, vowel team, or silent e. For a longer word, find its spoken syllables or compound-word parts. Read each chunk, blend the chunks, and check that the word makes sense.

worked_examples.text: Watch: In ship, sh is a digraph. It stays in one sound box, then blends with i and p.
Demo: In cake, the e is silent and helps a say its long sound.
Demo: Break sunset into sun | set, clap twice, then read the two chunks together.

hint_ladder: Say each part slowly before you blend.
Look for letters that work together as one sound.
Check whether the vowel is short, long, teamed, or helped by silent e.

guided_practice.prompt: Listen to flag. Which two letters form the beginning blend?
hints: Say flag slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of flag with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
guided_practice.explanation: In flag, f and l keep their own sounds. Blending /f/ and /l/ smoothly makes fl.
guided_practice.feedback: Correct — In flag, f and l keep their own sounds. Blending /f/ and /l/ smoothly makes fl.

question_audio.text: Listen to flag. Which two letters form the beginning blend?
question_audio.label: Read Question

guided_practice.prompt: The word ship begins with one sound spelled by two letters. Which letters spell that sound?
hints: Say ship slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of ship with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
guided_practice.explanation: The letters sh work together to spell /sh/ at the beginning of ship.
guided_practice.feedback: Correct — The letters sh work together to spell /sh/ at the beginning of ship.

question_audio.text: The word ship begins with one sound spelled by two letters. Which letters spell that sound?
question_audio.label: Read Question

guided_practice.prompt: Which beginning blend completes __uck to make truck?
hints: Say truck slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of truck with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
guided_practice.explanation: Adding tr to uck makes truck. You can hear both /t/ and /r/ in the blend tr.
guided_practice.feedback: Correct — Adding tr to uck makes truck. You can hear both /t/ and /r/ in the blend tr.

question_audio.text: Which beginning blend completes __uck to make truck?
question_audio.label: Read Question

guided_practice.prompt: Which word has a silent e that changes cap into a word with long a?
hints: Read cape aloud and listen to the vowel sound in the first syllable.
Look for an e at the end. A final silent e can help the earlier vowel say its long sound.
Try each choice, then choose the one that matches both the spelling pattern and the meaning of cape.
guided_practice.explanation: Cape ends with silent e. That e helps a say its long sound, /ā/.
guided_practice.feedback: Correct — Cape ends with silent e. That e helps a say its long sound, /ā/.

question_audio.text: Which word has a silent e that changes cap into a word with long a?
question_audio.label: Read Question

guided_practice.prompt: Use the tiles b–i–k–e. What word do they spell?
hints: Read bike aloud and listen to the vowel sound in the first syllable.
Look for an e at the end. A final silent e can help the earlier vowel say its long sound.
Try each choice, then choose the one that matches both the spelling pattern and the meaning of bike.
guided_practice.explanation: The final e is silent and makes i long, so b–i–k–e spells bike.
guided_practice.feedback: Correct — The final e is silent and makes i long, so b–i–k–e spells bike.

question_audio.text: Use the tiles b–i–k–e. What word do they spell?
question_audio.label: Read Question

checkpoint.prompt: In rain, which vowel team spells the long-a sound?
hints: Say rain slowly and listen for the vowel sound.
Find two neighboring vowel letters that work as a team; keep them together as you decode.
Test the vowel sound in each choice, then blend all of rain to confirm your answer.
checkpoint.explanation: The middle letters ai work as a team to spell long a in rain.
checkpoint.feedback: Correct — The middle letters ai work as a team to spell long a in rain.

question_audio.text: In rain, which vowel team spells the long-a sound?
question_audio.label: Read Question

checkpoint.prompt: Which break shows the two smaller words in sunset?
hints: Say sunset naturally and tap once for each spoken beat.
Match each spoken beat to a readable chunk; do not split a sound in an awkward place.
Blend the chunks back together. The result should sound exactly like sunset.
checkpoint.explanation: Sunset is a compound word made from sun and set, so the useful break is sun | set.
checkpoint.feedback: Correct — Sunset is a compound word made from sun and set, so the useful break is sun | set.

question_audio.text: Which break shows the two smaller words in sunset?
question_audio.label: Read Question

checkpoint.prompt: The frog sprang from a rock. Which beginning blend is in sprang?
hints: Say sprang slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of sprang with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
checkpoint.explanation: Sprang begins with the three-letter blend spr. You can hear /s/, /p/, and /r/.
checkpoint.feedback: Correct — Sprang begins with the three-letter blend spr. You can hear /s/, /p/, and /r/.

question_audio.text: The frog sprang from a rock. Which beginning blend is in sprang?
question_audio.label: Read Question

adaptive_question_bank.prompt: The frog sprang from a rock. Which beginning blend is in sprang?
hints: Say sprang slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of sprang with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
adaptive_question_bank.explanation: Sprang begins with the three-letter blend spr. You can hear /s/, /p/, and /r/.
adaptive_question_bank.feedback: Correct — Sprang begins with the three-letter blend spr. You can hear /s/, /p/, and /r/.

question_audio.text: The frog sprang from a rock. Which beginning blend is in sprang?
question_audio.label: Read Question

adaptive_question_bank.prompt: Which word begins with the same digraph as cheese?
hints: Say chase slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of chase with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
adaptive_question_bank.explanation: Cheese and chase both begin with the digraph ch.
adaptive_question_bank.feedback: Correct — Cheese and chase both begin with the digraph ch.

question_audio.text: Which word begins with the same digraph as cheese?
question_audio.label: Read Question

adaptive_question_bank.prompt: Choose the silent-e word that completes the sentence: The knight was __.
hints: Read brave aloud and listen to the vowel sound in the first syllable.
Look for an e at the end. A final silent e can help the earlier vowel say its long sound.
Try each choice, then choose the one that matches both the spelling pattern and the meaning of brave.
adaptive_question_bank.explanation: Brave fits the sentence and uses the a-consonant-silent-e pattern for long a.
adaptive_question_bank.feedback: Correct — Brave fits the sentence and uses the a-consonant-silent-e pattern for long a.

question_audio.text: Choose the silent-e word that completes the sentence: The knight was __.
question_audio.label: Read Question

adaptive_question_bank.prompt: A good night's sleep can help you __. Which vowel-team word completes the sentence?
hints: Say dream slowly and listen for the vowel sound.
Find two neighboring vowel letters that work as a team; keep them together as you decode.
Test the vowel sound in each choice, then blend all of dream to confirm your answer.
adaptive_question_bank.explanation: Dream fits the sentence. Its ea vowel team spells the long-e sound.
adaptive_question_bank.feedback: Correct — Dream fits the sentence. Its ea vowel team spells the long-e sound.

question_audio.text: A good night's sleep can help you __. Which vowel-team word completes the sentence?
question_audio.label: Read Question

adaptive_question_bank.prompt: Which chunks combine to make helmet?
hints: Say helmet naturally and tap once for each spoken beat.
Match each spoken beat to a readable chunk; do not split a sound in an awkward place.
Blend the chunks back together. The result should sound exactly like helmet.
adaptive_question_bank.explanation: Helmet has two syllables, hel | met. Blending those chunks makes the whole word.
adaptive_question_bank.feedback: Correct — Helmet has two syllables, hel | met. Blending those chunks makes the whole word.

question_audio.text: Which chunks combine to make helmet?
question_audio.label: Read Question

adaptive_question_bank.prompt: Read toast. Which vowel team spells the long-o sound?
hints: Say toast slowly and listen for the vowel sound.
Find two neighboring vowel letters that work as a team; keep them together as you decode.
Test the vowel sound in each choice, then blend all of toast to confirm your answer.
adaptive_question_bank.explanation: The letters oa work together to spell long o in toast.
adaptive_question_bank.feedback: Correct — The letters oa work together to spell long o in toast.

question_audio.text: Read toast. Which vowel team spells the long-o sound?
question_audio.label: Read Question

misconception_bank.blend_digraph_confusion: Learner may treat a blend like one sound or split a digraph that should stay together.
misconception_bank.silent_e_confusion: Learner may read a silent-e word with a short vowel or pronounce the final e.
misconception_bank.vowel_team_confusion: Learner may split vowel-team letters instead of reading the team as one sound.
misconception_bank.syllable_break_error: Learner may divide the word at the wrong place or miss a syllable when clapping.

review_bank.prompt: Say slide slowly. Which blend do you hear before the long i sound?
hints: Say slide slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of slide with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
review_bank.explanation: Slide begins /s/–/l/. Those two sounds blend together as sl before the long i.
review_bank.feedback: Correct — Slide begins /s/–/l/. Those two sounds blend together as sl before the long i.

question_audio.text: Say slide slowly. Which blend do you hear before the long i sound?
question_audio.label: Read Question

review_bank.prompt: What happens to the vowel sound when silent e changes plan to plane?
hints: Read plane aloud and listen to the vowel sound in the first syllable.
Look for an e at the end. A final silent e can help the earlier vowel say its long sound.
Try each choice, then choose the one that matches both the spelling pattern and the meaning of plane.
review_bank.explanation: In plane, silent e helps a say its name, the long-a sound.
review_bank.feedback: Correct — In plane, silent e helps a say its name, the long-a sound.

question_audio.text: What happens to the vowel sound when silent e changes plan to plane?
question_audio.label: Read Question

review_bank.prompt: Which vowel team completes l__f to make leaf?
hints: Say leaf slowly and listen for the vowel sound.
Find two neighboring vowel letters that work as a team; keep them together as you decode.
Test the vowel sound in each choice, then blend all of leaf to confirm your answer.
review_bank.explanation: The vowel team ea completes l–ea–f and spells the long-e sound in leaf.
review_bank.feedback: Correct — The vowel team ea completes l–ea–f and spells the long-e sound in leaf.

question_audio.text: Which vowel team completes l__f to make leaf?
question_audio.label: Read Question

review_bank.prompt: Say robot slowly. Which first syllable do you hear?
hints: Say robot naturally and tap once for each spoken beat.
Match each spoken beat to a readable chunk; do not split a sound in an awkward place.
Blend the chunks back together. The result should sound exactly like robot.
review_bank.explanation: Robot is read ro | bot. The first open syllable ro ends in a vowel sound.
review_bank.feedback: Correct — Robot is read ro | bot. The first open syllable ro ends in a vowel sound.

question_audio.text: Say robot slowly. Which first syllable do you hear?
question_audio.label: Read Question

review_bank.prompt: Which chunks combine to make helmet?
hints: Say helmet naturally and tap once for each spoken beat.
Match each spoken beat to a readable chunk; do not split a sound in an awkward place.
Blend the chunks back together. The result should sound exactly like helmet.
review_bank.explanation: Helmet has two syllables, hel | met. Blending those chunks makes the whole word.
review_bank.feedback: Correct — Helmet has two syllables, hel | met. Blending those chunks makes the whole word.

question_audio.text: Which chunks combine to make helmet?
question_audio.label: Read Question

game_theme.mission_intro: Collect sound clues, build words, and unlock the Advanced Phonics badge.

questions.prompt: Choose the digraph that completes __op to spell chop.
hints: Say chop slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of chop with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
questions.explanation: The digraph ch spells the first sound in chop, so ch plus op makes chop.
questions.feedback: Correct — The digraph ch spells the first sound in chop, so ch plus op makes chop.

question_audio.text: Choose the digraph that completes __op to spell chop.

questions.prompt: Say slide slowly. Which blend do you hear before the long i sound?
hints: Say slide slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of slide with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
questions.explanation: Slide begins /s/–/l/. Those two sounds blend together as sl before the long i.

question_audio.text: Say slide slowly. Which blend do you hear before the long i sound?
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.prompt: Which word begins with the digraph that spells /th/?
hints: Say thin slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of thin with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
questions.explanation: Thin begins with th, the two-letter spelling for /th/. The other words begin with /f/, /sh/, or /ch/.
questions.feedback: Correct — Thin begins with th, the two-letter spelling for /th/. The other words begin with /f/, /sh/, or /ch/.

question_audio.text: Which word begins with the digraph that spells /th/?
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.prompt: Brush starts with /b/ and /r/ blended together. Which letters show that blend?
hints: Say brush slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of brush with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
questions.explanation: The beginning sounds in brush are /b/ and /r/, so the blend is br.
questions.feedback: Correct — The beginning sounds in brush are /b/ and /r/, so the blend is br.

question_audio.text: Brush starts with /b/ and /r/ blended together. Which letters show that blend?
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.prompt: Which beginning blend turns ring into string?
hints: Say string slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of string with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
questions.explanation: Adding st before ring makes string. Both sounds in st can be heard.
questions.feedback: Correct — Adding st before ring makes string. Both sounds in st can be heard.

question_audio.text: Which beginning blend turns ring into string?
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.prompt: Read whale. Which digraph spells the sound at the beginning?
hints: Say whale slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of whale with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
questions.explanation: Wh is the beginning digraph in whale. The letters work together at the start of the word.
questions.feedback: Correct — Wh is the beginning digraph in whale. The letters work together at the start of the word.

question_audio.text: Read whale. Which digraph spells the sound at the beginning?
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.prompt: Blend /k/ and /l/, then add ip. Which word do you make?
hints: Say clip slowly. Pay special attention to the consonant sound or sounds at the beginning.
In a blend, you can hear each consonant sound. In a digraph, two letters work together to spell one sound.
Compare the beginning of clip with each choice, then blend the whole word to check it.
questions.explanation: Blending /k/ and /l/ gives cl; cl plus ip makes clip.
questions.feedback: Correct — Blending /k/ and /l/ gives cl; cl plus ip makes clip.

question_audio.text: Blend /k/ and /l/, then add ip. Which word do you make?
question_audio.label: Read Question

questions.explanation: Adding silent e to cub makes cube. The u changes from short /ŭ/ to long /ū/.

story.text: Listen to this page. You are starting an English mission about Advanced Phonics and Word Analysis. Look at the story and get ready to read carefully. Think about the sounds, words, sentences, or details that matter. Press Start Mission when you are ready.

lesson.text: This lesson teaches Advanced Phonics and Word Analysis. Look for the reading or writing pattern on the page. Listen for word parts, important details, or sentence choices that help you decide. Remember to reread before you answer. Press Next to continue.
